claim: Larry Hodges identifies forehand pendulum, reverse pendulum, forehand tomahawk and backhand sidespin as the four dominant tournament service motions; pendulum is most common because spin breaks away from the receiver's backhand.
quote: Because the spin breaks away from receiver's backhand, it's awkward to receive.

claim: Every player in the men's top 10 uses a pendulum-style forehand serve as their primary opener; the chop (backspin) serve is rated the safest first serve to learn.
quote: Most popular among professionals; every single one of top 10 players uses it.

claim: Expert Table Tennis names chop, pendulum and reverse pendulum/shovel as the three serves every player should master because together they cover the full spin spectrum.
quote: I cannot stress how important it is to be able to perform every kind of spin possible for your serves.

claim: At amateur level, deception between heavy backspin and no-spin from an identical pendulum motion wins more points than raw spin alone; contact near the racket handle (low blade speed) produces a credible no-spin variant.
quote: The power of a serve doesn't lie in its spin, it lies in its deceptiveness.

claim: Tom Lodziak: pairing a heavy-backspin pendulum with a same-motion no-spin variant traps opponents who reuse their push stroke, popping the no-spin ball up for a third-ball attack.
quote: It's the variation between heavy spin and no spin which causes all the problems.

claim: Tom Lodziak argues amateur-level players underuse long serves; deep, fast serves with spin disguise overwhelm passive opponents who rely on pushing or chopping.
quote: Long serves can give lower level players nightmares.

claim: Lodziak recommends using long serves up to 80% of the time against weaker opponents who panic on fast deep serves, while dropping to mostly-short against opponents who attack long serves cleanly.
quote: Use long serves a lot, as much as 80% of the time.

claim: Larry Hodges: a fast no-spin serve aimed at the opponent's playing elbow (the forehand/backhand crossover) is a near-free point at amateur level, used 2–3 times per game to keep it surprising.
quote: This is a free point about half the time against players rated under 2000.

claim: Expert Table Tennis frames the tomahawk as one of the most powerful weapons at beginner-to-intermediate level because most amateurs are not grooved against its motion; a short tomahawk to the forehand draws errors or pop-ups.
quote: One of the most powerful weapons in table tennis today, especially among beginner and intermediate players.

claim: Larry Hodges (Butterfly): adding reverse versions of your main serve makes opponents uncomfortable even when the reverse is less polished, simply because they have not seen it.
quote: They might never become equal in effectiveness as the regular version, but at the very least they give you a variation that the opponent isn't used to.

claim: Lodziak's five elements of a strong service game: spin, placement (short/half-long/long across forehand/middle/backhand), variation, deception, and recovery into the third-ball position.
quote: Serve short, half-long and long, all to the forehand side, backhand side and middle.

claim: PingSunday: the canonical amateur-winning pattern is short backspin serve, opponent pushes long, server loops the third ball; many club players never solve this loop.
quote: The most basic and common third-ball attack in table tennis is to serve short backspin, and loop the receiver's push return.

claim: TableTennisDaily Academy's reverse-pendulum drill: isolate the wrist square-to-table first, then add forearm and waist rotation, then change spin by adjusting contact point under or around the ball.
quote: First start square to the table which forces you to isolate the wrist on it's own without any body movement.

claim: Coach Ferenc Horvath's seven-serve curriculum (pendulum, reverse pendulum, backspin, no-spin, hook, tomahawk, backhand) defines the realistic full toolkit for serious amateurs.
quote: Used by many top amateurs and pro players.

claim: Legal-serve checklist: open palm, ball stationary above the flat palm behind the end-line, near-vertical toss of at least 16 cm with no spin, ball visible to the receiver throughout — break any rule and the point is gone before the rally starts.
quote: The server shall project the ball near vertically upwards, without imparting spin, so that it rises at least 16cm after leaving the palm of the free hand.
